content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers 30 (NUM_030)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 30:1 -- "This [is] the word that" vs. "This is the word which" vs. "This is the word that"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "This [is] the word that YHWH has commanded" (brackets around implied copula) |
| LOGOS | "This is the word which YHWH has commanded" (uses "which") |
| PNEUMA | "This is the word that YHWH has commanded" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "This is the word that YHWH has commanded" -- follows PNEUMA. The copula "is" is naturally implied and need not be bracketed. "That" is preferred over "which" for a defining relative clause in standard English usage. Minor divergence.

---

### 30:2 -- "vows a vow" vs. "makes a vow"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "When a man vows a vow to YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "When a man vows a vow to YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "When a man makes a vow to YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "When a man vows a vow to YHWH" -- follows EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew uses the cognate accusative construction (yidor neder), which the SOPHIA Bible preserves. PNEUMA's "makes a vow" loses this Hebrew idiom.

---

### 30:2 -- "binding" vs. "binding obligation" vs. "solemn obligation"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to bind a binding upon his soul" |
| LOGOS | "to bind a binding obligation upon his soul" |
| PNEUMA | "binding himself under a solemn obligation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to bind a binding upon his soul" -- follows EMET. The Hebrew issar is rendered "binding" to preserve the cognate force with the verb asar ("to bind"). LOGOS adds "obligation" for clarity; PNEUMA restructures entirely with "solemn obligation" and moves the reflexive sense into "binding himself." The cognate accusative pattern ("bind a binding") parallels "vow a vow" and should be preserved.

---

### 30:2 -- "profane his word" vs. "break his word"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he shall not profane his word" |
| LOGOS | "he shall not profane his word" |
| PNEUMA | "he must not break his word" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he shall not profane his word" -- follows EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew yachel (Hiphil of chalal) means "to profane, desecrate," not merely "to break." PNEUMA's "break" is a dynamic simplification that loses the theological weight. Both EMET and LOGOS correctly preserve the gravity of the Hebrew.

---

### 30:2 -- "goes out from his mouth" vs. "proceeds from his mouth" vs. "has come out of his mouth"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "according to all that goes out from his mouth he shall do" |
| LOGOS | "according to all that proceeds from his mouth he shall do" |
| PNEUMA | "He must do everything that has come out of his mouth" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "according to all that goes out from his mouth he shall do" -- follows EMET. "Goes out" is the most natural rendering of the Hebrew yatsa. LOGOS's "proceeds" is slightly more formal but acceptable. PNEUMA restructures the entire clause, moving the verb forward and using past tense "has come out," which alters the temporal sense from a general principle to a completed action. The Hebrew is a participial construction expressing ongoing applicability.

---

### 30:3 -- "And when a woman vows a vow to YHWH and binds a binding in the house of her father" vs. "And when a young woman still living in her father's house makes a vow to YHWH"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And when a woman vows a vow to YHWH, and binds a binding in the house of her father in her youth" |
| LOGOS | "And when a woman vows a vow to YHWH and binds a binding obligation in the house of her father in her youth" |
| PNEUMA | "And when a young woman still living in her father's house makes a vow to YHWH or binds herself under a solemn obligation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when a woman vows a vow to YHWH and binds a binding in the house of her father in her youth" -- follows EMET/LOGOS clause order. PNEUMA relocates "in her father's house" to the front of the verse and renders "young woman" instead of "woman...in her youth," which collapses two distinct Hebrew elements (ishah and bin'ureha) into a single phrase. The Hebrew first identifies her as ishah ("woman"), then specifies the circumstance ("in her youth, in the house of her father"). Also, PNEUMA uses "or" between vow and binding; the Hebrew has waw-consecutive ("and"), not "or."

---

### 30:4 -- "silent to her" vs. "silent toward her" vs. "says nothing to her"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "her father is silent to her" |
| LOGOS | "her father is silent toward her" |
| PNEUMA | "her father says nothing to her" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "her father is silent to her" -- follows EMET. The Hebrew hecharish lah is literally "was silent to her." LOGOS's "toward" is a minor preposition variation. PNEUMA's "says nothing to her" is a dynamic equivalent that shifts from describing the father's state (silence) to describing his speech (none), losing the deliberate legal concept of silence as tacit consent.

---

### 30:5 -- "And if" vs. "But if" / "restrains" vs. "overrules" / "all her vows and her bindings...shall not stand" vs. "none of her vows...shall stand"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if her father restrains her in the day of his hearing, all her vows and her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall not stand" |
| LOGOS | "But if her father restrains her on the day he hears, none of her vows or her binding obligations which she has bound upon her soul shall stand" |
| PNEUMA | "But if her father overrules her on the day he hears of it, none of her vows or binding obligations that she placed upon herself shall stand" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But if her father restrains her on the day of his hearing, none of her vows or her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall stand" -- Multiple divergences here: (1) "And if" (EMET) vs. "But if" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): The Hebrew v'im introduces a contrastive conditional; "But if" better captures this. (2) "restrains" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "overrules" (PNEUMA): The Hebrew heni (Hiphil of nua) means "to restrain, hinder"; "overrules" implies judicial authority not present in the root. (3) "all...shall not stand" (EMET) vs. "none...shall stand" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): Both are logically equivalent, but "none...shall stand" follows more natural English syntax. (4) "in the day of his hearing" (EMET) vs. "on the day he hears" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): The Hebrew b'yom shom'o is literally "in the day of his hearing"; the consensus uses "on the day of his hearing" as a compromise, retaining the nominal construction.

---

### 30:5 -- "YHWH will forgive her" vs. "YHWH will release her from guilt"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "YHWH will forgive her" |
| LOGOS | "YHWH will forgive her" |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH will release her from guilt" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "YHWH will forgive her" -- follows EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew salach is the standard verb for divine forgiveness. PNEUMA's "release her from guilt" is interpretive expansion. This disagreement recurs in vv. 8 and 12 as well; the same resolution applies throughout.

---

### 30:6 -- "she is indeed [given] to a husband" vs. "she is indeed given to a husband" vs. "she marries a husband"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if she is indeed [given] to a husband, and her vows [are] upon her, or the utterance of her lips which she has bound upon her soul" |
| LOGOS | "And if she is indeed given to a husband, and her vows are upon her, or a rash utterance of her lips by which she has bound her soul" |
| PNEUMA | "And if she marries a husband while her vows are upon her, or while a rash promise from her lips still binds her" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if she is indeed given to a husband, and her vows are upon her, or the utterance of her lips by which she has bound her soul" -- The infinitive absolute hayo tihyeh is preserved with "is indeed." EMET brackets "[given]" as an implied word; LOGOS includes it without brackets; both are acceptable since the Hebrew idiom hayo tihyeh l'ish requires a supplement in English. PNEUMA's "marries" collapses the emphatic construction entirely. For "utterance": EMET has "the utterance"; LOGOS has "a rash utterance"; PNEUMA has "a rash promise." The Hebrew mivta means "utterance" -- the rashness is inherent in the root bata but is not marked by an adjective in the text. "The utterance of her lips" is adopted. LOGOS's "by which" is preferred over EMET's "which" for the relative clause, as the binding is accomplished by means of the utterance.

---

### 30:7 -- "and in the day of his hearing he is silent to her" vs. "and is silent toward her on the day he hears" vs. "and says nothing to her on the day he learns of it"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and her husband hears, and in the day of his hearing he is silent to her" |
| LOGOS | "and her husband hears and is silent toward her on the day he hears" |
| PNEUMA | "and her husband hears of it and says nothing to her on the day he learns of it" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and her husband hears, and on the day of his hearing he is silent to her" -- follows EMET's clause structure, which mirrors the Hebrew syntax most closely. PNEUMA adds "of it" (not in Hebrew) and substitutes "learns" for "hears" and "says nothing" for "is silent." LOGOS places "on the day he hears" at the end; EMET places it mid-clause as the Hebrew does.

---

### 30:8 -- "And if in the day of" vs. "But if on the day" / "restrains...and he annuls" vs. "restrains...then he shall annul" vs. "overrules...then he nullifies"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if in the day of her husband's hearing he restrains her, and he annuls her vow...then YHWH will forgive her" |
| LOGOS | "But if on the day her husband hears he restrains her, then he shall annul her vow...and YHWH will forgive her" |
| PNEUMA | "But if on the day her husband hears of it he overrules her, then he nullifies the vow...And YHWH will release her from guilt" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But if on the day of her husband's hearing he restrains her, then he annuls her vow which is upon her and the utterance of her lips by which she has bound her soul; and YHWH will forgive her" -- "But if" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for the contrastive conditional. "Restrains" follows EMET/LOGOS over PNEUMA's "overrules." "Annuls" follows EMET/LOGOS over PNEUMA's "nullifies." The Hebrew hefer means "to break, annul" -- "annul" is the most precise legal term.

---

### 30:9 -- "And the vow of a widow and one divorced" vs. "And the vow of a widow or a divorced woman" vs. "As for the vow of a widow or a divorced woman"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the vow of a widow and one divorced -- all that she has bound upon her soul shall stand upon her" |
| LOGOS | "And the vow of a widow or a divorced woman -- everything by which she has bound her soul shall stand against her" |
| PNEUMA | "As for the vow of a widow or a divorced woman -- every obligation she has bound upon herself shall stand against her" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the vow of a widow or a divorced woman -- all that she has bound upon her soul shall stand against her" -- (1) "and" vs. "or": The Hebrew u-g'rushah uses waw, which can be conjunctive or disjunctive; "or" is preferred here since either category independently qualifies. (2) "one divorced" (EMET) vs. "a divorced woman" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): The Hebrew g'rushah is a feminine passive participle; "a divorced woman" is clearer in English. (3) "upon her" (EMET) vs. "against her" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): The Hebrew aleiha can mean either "upon" or "against"; "against" better captures the legal force of binding obligation. (4) "all that" (EMET) vs. "everything by which" (LOGOS) vs. "every obligation" (PNEUMA): "All that she has bound upon her soul" follows EMET's more literal construction.

---

### 30:10 -- "And if in the house of her husband she vowed" vs. "And if she vowed in the house of her husband" vs. "And if she made her vow while in her husband's house"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if in the house of her husband she vowed, or she bound a binding upon her soul with an oath" |
| LOGOS | "And if she vowed in the house of her husband, or bound a binding obligation upon her soul with an oath" |
| PNEUMA | "And if she made her vow while in her husband's house, or bound herself under a solemn obligation with an oath" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if in the house of her husband she vowed, or she bound a binding upon her soul with an oath" -- follows EMET's word order, which mirrors the Hebrew (b'veit ishah nadarah). The Hebrew places "in the house of her husband" first for emphasis. LOGOS and PNEUMA reorder to standard English syntax. PNEUMA's "made her vow" and "bound herself under a solemn obligation" are dynamic expansions.

---

### 30:11 -- "[and] did not restrain her" vs. "he did not restrain her" vs. "he did not overrule her"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "[and] did not restrain her" |
| LOGOS | "he did not restrain her" |
| PNEUMA | "he did not overrule her" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and did not restrain her" -- follows EMET's structure. The Hebrew lo heni'ah is a simple negation without an explicit pronoun (the subject is carried from the previous clause). EMET's bracketed "[and]" is normalized to "and." PNEUMA's "overrule" is again replaced with "restrain" per the standing resolution on heni.

---

### 30:12 -- "surely annulling annuls" vs. "indeed annuls" vs. "decisively nullifies"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "if her husband surely annulling annuls them in the day of his hearing" |
| LOGOS | "if her husband indeed annuls them on the day he hears" |
| PNEUMA | "if her husband decisively nullifies them on the day he hears of them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "if her husband surely annulling annuls them on the day of his hearing" -- The Hebrew hafer yafer is an infinitive absolute construction. Per the binding convention, infinitive absolute is preserved with doubling: "surely annulling annuls." LOGOS reduces to "indeed annuls," which captures the emphasis but loses the doubled form. PNEUMA renders "decisively nullifies," a dynamic equivalent that drops both the infinitive absolute pattern and the Hebrew verb hefer in favor of "nullify."

---

### 30:12 -- "all that goes out from her lips" vs. "whatever proceeds from her lips" vs. "nothing that came from her lips"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all that goes out from her lips concerning her vows and concerning the binding of her soul shall not stand" |
| LOGOS | "whatever proceeds from her lips concerning her vows or concerning the binding obligation of her soul shall not stand" |
| PNEUMA | "nothing that came from her lips -- whether vows or binding obligations -- shall stand" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "all that goes out from her lips concerning her vows and concerning the binding of her soul shall not stand" -- follows EMET. "Goes out" is the natural rendering of yatsa. LOGOS's "proceeds" is slightly more formal. PNEUMA restructures with "nothing...shall stand" (logically equivalent to "all...shall not stand") and uses "whether...or" for the Hebrew l- prefix. The EMET construction most closely mirrors the Hebrew syntax.

---

### 30:13 -- "oath of binding to afflict [the] soul" vs. "binding oath to afflict the soul" vs. "sworn obligation to deny herself"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Every vow and every oath of binding to afflict [the] soul -- her husband may cause it to stand, and her husband may annul it" |
| LOGOS | "Every vow and every binding oath to afflict the soul -- her husband may establish it, or her husband may annul it" |
| PNEUMA | "Every vow and every sworn obligation to deny herself -- her husband may uphold it, or her husband may nullify it" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Every vow and every binding oath to afflict the soul -- her husband may cause it to stand, and her husband may annul it" -- (1) "oath of binding" (EMET) vs. "binding oath" (LOGOS) vs. "sworn obligation" (PNEUMA): The Hebrew shvu'at issar is "oath of binding"; EMET's rendering preserves the construct chain. LOGOS inverts to "binding oath" which is natural English but reverses the Hebrew. The consensus adopts "binding oath" as a compromise -- it retains both key terms while reading naturally. (2) "to afflict [the] soul" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "to deny herself" (PNEUMA): The Hebrew l'annot nefesh is literally "to afflict soul." PNEUMA's "deny herself" is interpretive. (3) "cause it to stand" (EMET) vs. "establish it" (LOGOS) vs. "uphold it" (PNEUMA): The Hebrew heqim is Hiphil of qum, "to cause to stand." EMET is most literal; LOGOS and PNEUMA use English equivalents. The consensus uses "cause it to stand." (4) "and" (EMET) vs. "or" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): The Hebrew waw here is disjunctive -- the husband may do one or the other. However, "and" in context also works (listing both options). The consensus retains "and" following EMET.

---

### 30:14 -- "is indeed silent" vs. "is altogether silent" vs. "says absolutely nothing"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if her husband is indeed silent to her from day to day" |
| LOGOS | "And if her husband is altogether silent toward her from day to day" |
| PNEUMA | "But if her husband says absolutely nothing to her from one day to the next" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if her husband is indeed silent to her from day to day" -- The Hebrew hacharesh yacharish is another infinitive absolute construction. "Indeed" captures the emphasis while "altogether" (LOGOS) suggests totality rather than emphasis. PNEUMA's "says absolutely nothing" is a full dynamic restructuring. For "from day to day": EMET and LOGOS render mi-yom el-yom literally; PNEUMA renders "from one day to the next," which adds "one" and "the next" not present in Hebrew.

---

### 30:14 -- "causes to stand" vs. "establishes" vs. "upheld"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "then he causes to stand all her vows...he has caused them to stand, because he was silent to her in the day of his hearing" |
| LOGOS | "then he establishes all her vows...he has established them, because he was silent toward her on the day he heard" |
| PNEUMA | "then he has upheld all her vows...He has upheld them, because he said nothing to her on the day he heard them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "then he causes to stand all her vows...he has caused them to stand, because he was silent to her on the day of his hearing" -- "causes to stand" (EMET) most literally renders the Hiphil of qum. LOGOS's "establishes" and PNEUMA's "upheld" are acceptable English equivalents but lose the concrete imagery of the Hebrew. The pattern of literal Hiphil rendering is maintained for consistency.

---

### 30:15 -- "surely annulling annuls them after his hearing" vs. "indeed annuls them after he has heard" vs. "nullifies them some time after he first heard them"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if he surely annulling annuls them after his hearing, then he shall bear her iniquity" |
| LOGOS | "But if he indeed annuls them after he has heard, then he shall bear her iniquity" |
| PNEUMA | "But if he nullifies them some time after he first heard them, then he shall bear her guilt" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But if he surely annulling annuls them after his hearing, then he shall bear her iniquity" -- (1) Infinitive absolute hafer yafer preserved as "surely annulling annuls" per binding convention. (2) "And if" (EMET) vs. "But if" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): "But if" is preferred for the contrastive condition. (3) "after his hearing" (EMET) vs. "after he has heard" (LOGOS) vs. "some time after he first heard them" (PNEUMA): PNEUMA adds interpretive expansion ("some time," "first") not in the Hebrew. "After his hearing" follows the nominal construction of the Hebrew acharei shom'o. (4) "her iniquity" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "her guilt" (PNEUMA): The Hebrew avonah means "iniquity, guilt, punishment for iniquity." "Iniquity" is the more traditional and theologically precise rendering.

---

### 30:16 -- "between a father and his daughter, in her youth in the house of her father" vs. "between a father and his daughter in her youth in the house of her father" vs. "between a father and his daughter while she is young, still living in her father's house"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "between a father and his daughter, in her youth in the house of her father" |
| LOGOS | "between a father and his daughter in her youth in the house of her father" |
| PNEUMA | "between a father and his daughter while she is young, still living in her father's house" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "between a father and his daughter, in her youth in the house of her father" -- follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA expands "in her youth" to "while she is young" and adds "still living in," which are clarifying additions not present in the Hebrew. The nominal phrase bin'ureha b'veit aviha is literally "in her youth in the house of her father."

---

### 30:17 -- Versification disagreement: inclusion vs. omission
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | Marks v. 17 as "[This is the end of the chapter in the Hebrew versification]" -- does not translate it |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying," |
| PNEUMA | Does not include v. 17 (follows English 16-verse numbering) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying," -- follows LOGOS. The Leningrad Codex includes this verse as 30:17 (MT versification). It is the opening formula of the Midianite war command that continues in chapter 31. EMET acknowledged 17 verses but did not translate the final one; PNEUMA followed an English versification that omits it. Since the SOPHIA Bible follows MT versification, the verse is included as translated by LOGOS.

---

*End of Disagreement Log for Numbers 30. Total: 17 disagreement entries covering all 16 substantive verses plus the versification dispute on v. 17.*
